sentences.text: Well, it-- as I said, it was like a little government looking after the affairs of the ghetto.
And I remember there was a man called Barash, who was head of the Judenrat.
And from what I can remember, I've heard since he was quite a, a controversial figure.
I don't think he actually collaborated with the Germans.
I mean, right through the war, there were people in the ghettos and in the camps who were actual-- who are Jewish and they were actual collaborators with the Germans.
But I don't think Barash was.
He probably wanted the best deal under the circumstances for the people in the ghetto.
But he also had to placate the Germans many times.
And a lot of people felt that he did the wrong thing.
But I don't think that he was a collaborator.
I'm not sure.
I think he just worked as an accountant in the, in the office there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- there was-- when the liquidation of the ghetto started, there was an uprising in, in the ghetto of Bialystok.

Not on the scale of the Warsaw ghetto, but I would imagine that right from the beginning of the establishment of the ghetto, these resistance workers must have trained and planned for when eventually they would have to start the uprising.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they probably would have got ammunition and guns and that from the outside of the ghetto.
But it wasn't easy.
Because the, the Poles didn't help the-- they were, they actually collaborated with the Germans.
And they were very anti-Jewish.
So there were Polish guards in the camp as well in the ghetto?
In the ghetto?
Yes.
Yes, there were.
Probably, yes.
Well, I don't remember hearing about it.
Because I was seven, eight years old.
But I'm sure my parents would have known about it.
They weren't actually members of the resistance.
But they would have heard about what was happening.
A lot of people at that stage, at that time in the ghetto, started building bunkers.
And-- because they felt that the time would come when the Germans would start rounding up people, men, or taking elderly away or children, which was their, their, their sort of plan.
And we had one in our, in our block of flats.
Well, it was actually very, very securely built.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just know-- when, when I, when I visualize our flats, I know that it was sort of on the right-hand side of the staircase.
It was eventually not even used.
Because when the liquidation started in August 1943, the Germans just started rounding up the whole population of the ghetto.
So I don't think that the bunker was actually used.
But I remember before, before we, we built our bunker, there was a disturbance in the ghetto.
And quite a few people were taken away.
And we went to a neighboring block of flats into a bunker.
And we stayed there probably a few days.
And I have this terrible recollection.
We, of course, had to sit there very quietly.
And there was a baby in the bunker.
And the baby started to cry.
And they put a pillow on the baby's head.
And the baby suffocated.
But, but it was either the baby crying or lots and lots of people being, being discovered and rounded up.
So it was, was pretty horrible.
Just a few days.
And then at one stage, my family decided that maybe I should be smuggled out of the ghetto and go to a Polish family.
And my uncle had this nurse, a Polish Catholic nurse, who he had worked with in the, in the hospital outside the ghetto.
And he contacted her and asked her whether she would look after me.
So I was smuggled out of the ghetto.
I can't remember how, but I was.
And I went with this girl to the country where she lived with her father.
And she didn't tell her father that I was Jewish.
She just said, oh, she's a cousin of a friend of mine where I work in the hospital.
And I was there for a few days.
And she already started teaching me Catholic prayers.
And then she could see it didn't work out.
Because her father started getting suspicious.
And she thought that I wouldn't be safe, and she wouldn't be safe.
So she let me go.
And I went back to the ghetto.
I think they would've been very distressed.
Because they seemed-- they probably thought that I had a more of a chance of survival outside the ghetto, as in living as an, as an, as an Aryan than I would.
Because our, our future in the ghetto was very, very unsafe, as I said before.
People knew that the ghetto wouldn't last forever.
At that time Germany-- in those early days, Germany was doing very well in the war.
And they were winning the war.
And I think people were very, very depressed about the outlook for the future.
That was February 1943.
Came back out.
And, and we stayed in the ghetto till August 1943.
Yes.
Yes, everything as was.
Yes.
And on the 16th of August 1943, the Germans started giving orders for people to leave their homes and to be, and to be rounded up in a huge empty place just outside the ghetto.
We weren't given very much time.
So everybody just took their-- just their personal belongings.
I remember my auntie put on three dresses, because she wasn't allowed to carry too much.
So she put on three dresses, one of which I've got.
And we'll see it later on.
Pardon me.
And so we went-- pardon me.
So we went to this-- my, my mother, my father, my grandmother, and I went to this huge place where the whole population of, of the ghetto was taken.
Oh, I don't remember.
But my aunt and my uncle and my cousin went to the Jewish hospital.
Because my uncle felt that as long as there was anybody left in the ghetto, they would need a doctor.
My aunt was a trained nurse.
And their daughter went with them.
So they went to the, to the Jewish hospital.
And as I remember, as we were led through the streets of the ghetto to the outside, there was shooting.
There was blood in the street.
And the, the Bialystok Ghetto Uprising had started.
So we were taken to this huge place.
It was August.
It was in the middle of summer.
From what I can remember, 1943 had a very, very hot summer in Poland.
And we were sitting there.
And then an order came that all the children were to be rounded up.
They, they sort of came with loudspeakers.
And they said-- and this was a trick the, the Germans used on the Jews all the time.
We're, we're taking away your children so they can be exchanged for, from, for German prisoners of war in Palestine.
And whether you believed it or not, this was an order from the Germans.
And you had to go.
So my parents discussed it.
And my father said to my mother, look, these children will need some women to be looked after, so you volunteer.
At least you will be with your daughter.
And we will meet up sometime.
Nobody knew what was going to, to happen.
So we were-- we went back to the ghetto.
There were about 1,000 children, little ones, babies, teenagers, six-, seven-, eight-year-olds.
And my mother was with me.
So that was the first day of the liquidation of the ghetto.
We went back to the ghetto.
And as it happened, the children were taken to a block of flats which was right opposite the Jewish hospital where my aunt and uncle were.
And I knew that.
We just sat around in this flat for a day or so.
And then the next day, the Germans took all the women away.
So that was-- the day before was the last time I saw my father.
And that day was the last time that I saw my mother.
The last thing I remember, my mother had a brown overcoat, which she-- although it was very warm, she had it on her arm.
She gave it to me and said, look, you might be cold at some stage.
You'll be, you'll be traveling somewhere.
You will need it.
So I remember sitting there huddled in a corner clutching this coat, covered with this coat, clutching this coat.
And that was the last thing I had from my mother, which is since-- was taken away from me.
And I really felt very, very alone, very lonely, very terrified.
Now, at-- what I didn't know was that there was a little-- there was a drama going on in the hospital with my aunt and uncle.
My cousin was 15 years old.
She had a boyfriend in the city.
And he worked in a factory.
And at one stage, there was a rumor that the Germans would leave a skeleton population in the ghetto of young factory workers who would produce things for the Third Reich.
And this, this boyfriend was in this, in this factory.
And when he gave his name he said, but he's got a wife who is in the, in the Jewish hospital with her parents.
And he bribed a German guard to go to the hospital to get her.
The German guard arrived.

And my aunt and uncle had to make a, a spitting, a very, very quick decision whether to let her go with young people where she would have had a little bit more chance of survival or to stay with her parents and probably eventually be exterminated.
So they gave her American dollars in case she ever needed them and the address of our-- because we had family in Australia who migrated here in 1926.
And they let her go.

And they didn't realize, they didn't know till quite some time later, that when she arrived into the factory and she was, she was with her boyfriend that another order came from Berlin to disband the factory.
And she was taken-- she-- that, that whole group were taken to Majdanek and to Auschwitz.
This-- some of this group organized an escape from the camp.
They were captured.
And they were shot.
But we didn't know that till after the war.
Now, my aunt and uncle then heard that there were children opposite them in this block of flats.
So my aunt bribed a German guard to go across the road and to get me.
It was quite, quite a miracle that out of 1,000 children, he found me.
And on my uncle's papers, there was Dr.
Treyvish, wife, and daughter.
And there was no age specified.
So I became their daughter for the remainder of the war and for the remainder of their lives.
I was their daughter.
I was later on formally adopted by them.
Now, the fate of those children we also didn't know till after the war.
They were all taken to Theresienstadt.
They were put into a barrack.
They, they were starved.
Some of the older ones then started a little uprising.
And they were all shot or, or burnt alive.
So I am in fact, the only child survivor of the, of the Bialystok ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went over to join my aunt and uncle in the hospital.
And we must have stayed there for another five, six days.
And then the Germans came, rounded up-- they probably shot all the, all the inmates of the hospital or the patients.
And they rounded up the doctors and the nursing staff.
And we were put on a train.
Because at that, at that stage, there were very, very few people left in the ghetto.
Most of the people who were like my father in this, in this empty square or whatever-- in this-- yes, were put onto trains.
And they were taken to Treblinka.
And they were exterminated.
Now, we were also put on a train.
And that train was just something horrific.
We were herded like cattle.
They were cattle trains.
And we were-- there were probably about 100 in every carriage.
You couldn't move.
You couldn't stand up.
You couldn't sit.
You had no toilet facilities.
We were hungry.
We were thirsty.
As I said before, it was very hot.
On the way, we stopped at various stations.
And we-- and people-- and the Polish, the Polish population of that area all came to the trains just to see how the Jews were being transported.
And people were throwing jewelry to the Poles, begging them for a, for a sip of water.
And they just laughed and didn't give us any water.
At one stage, the whole train stopped outside a huge gate.
And on top of the gate, there were huge doors.
And on top of the, the doors was a sign called Treblinka [NON-ENGLISH], which means Treblinka Jewish labor camp.
But of course, Treblinka was not a labor camp.
It was an extermination camp.
But to the outside world, the Germans presented themselves as benefactors of the Jews.
And they let them be taken from the ghetto into places where they could work.
It was just another lie that they used on the population.
We stopped there.
And we-- rumors had already started about Treblinka while we were still in the ghetto.
And the people were screaming and shouting.
Because we were all worried that we would be the ones which carriages would stop in Treblinka.
But by some-- I mean, there were lots and lots of miracles in our lives.
That, that's all I can say.
Just fate said that we were to go on.
And our train moved on.
And we-- eventually, after stopping in a few places, we were taken to a camp called Blizyn, which is near Radom in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had-- there was quite a big gathering of people there already.
And we came as a group.
There must've been about 30 or 40 of us, doctors and their wives and their children and nursing staff and their families.
[DRINKS]
[POINTING] We saw barracks.
This was not a camp.
This was not an extermination camp.
This is a working camp.
There were no ovens.
And in every camp, there were-- they-- there was a, an empty sort of like a square, which was next to the barracks.
And we sat in that square with our belongings.
And then the Germans came and took all the jewelry, all the clothing.
My aunt was left in just the dress that-- she was told to take off her, her clothes.
And when they realized that she had three dresses, they only left her one, which is one that I will show you later.
So we were eventually put into barracks.
Men were segregated from women.
There were men's barracks.
There were ladies', women's barracks.
And the men went out on road works.
And the women went-- worked in a factory which, which manufactured German uniforms.
And at that stage, my aunt decided that-- I was nine years old.
And at nine, I could easily be taken away for extermination.
Because they only wanted people who were useful.
So she-- when she arrived-- when they, we arrived in that camp, she put my age up to 12.
Because at 12, I could already be part of the workforce.
And I used to go with her every morning to this workshop.
There were workshops.
And I was taught how to sew buttons on German uniforms.
And I was taught so well that for years later, since then, every time I sew on a button, I remember sitting there learning how to sew on buttons on German uniforms.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle was, was not, was not admitted to the hospital to work as a doctor in that camp.
Because they had too many doctors there already.
So he was sent out on the road works.
And somehow, he seemed to get into trouble there.
Every day you had to go past a guard search when you, when you came back from the work into the camp.
And once, they found a carrot in his pocket.
Somebody had planted it.
And he got a beating.
Or he got a-- a beating.
Also, at that stage, they-- the Germans came and took away all the children from the ghetto for extermination.
I was hidden.
And besides, I was already 12.
So I wasn't considered a child.
So I survived that.
Yes, I did.
I was small.
I was skinny.
But I think everybody was, everybody was undernourished.
So they didn't seem to notice very much that I, that I really didn't look 12.
As I said, my uncle-- my aunt was very resourceful, very enterprising.
At that stage, although she'd always been the weaker person before the war, she became the stronger of the two of them.
My uncle seemed to just lose his spirit, his will to live.
And she really supported him very much in this time.
And she, she encouraged him to go on.
But she had terrible foreboding that things-- once, he was caught with something else.
And he was called-- every morning, they had this gathering outside.
It was called an appell.
Everybody had to stand.
And it was already getting a bit cold at that time.
It was October, November.
And we had to stand for hours.
And the Germans called out people who had committed some small misdemeanor or something.
And these people were publicly punished.
They were flogged.
They were shot.
And one day, my uncle was called.
And my aunt thought, that's, that's the end of it.
And she actually fainted.
And the women propped her up.
Because if you, if you fell, they shot you.
So the women all, all around her propped her up.
And they said, look, your husband's coming back.
He, he was-- he-- they, they released him.
So she said, oh, he's probably coming back to say goodbye to me.
And they said, no, he's coming back to stand with the men where he stood before.
For some reason or another, he wasn't shot.
After that, my auntie decided that if we didn't get out of this place, he would go.
There, there wasn't much future for him.
So she went to the head of the camp.
This is how enterprising she was.
And she said, look, my husband is a doctor, a very good doctor.
If there is ever any other camp which is in need of a doctor, please consider him.
And this is exactly what happened.
There was a new camp being established in Tschenstochau where there were 500 men.
And they needed a doctor.
So on the 3rd of December, 1943, we were taken in a, in a truck from Blizyn to Tschenstochau.
And we arrived there.
And there were only men.
And I could hear them say, yes, now there are two-- [GERMAN], two women.
And I was so proud of myself.
Because I was called a woman.
So we arrived in that camp.
And my uncle straight away started working as a doctor.
The conditions, the conditions for, for medical purposes were just terrible.
He had to operate.
He had to perform emergency operations.
He had to pull out teeth.
He really was a dentist, a gynecologist, a surgeon, a GP all in one.
Can I go to the toilet?
